questions.question: The law of war is also known by what other name?
options: Law of Military Engagement (LOME)
Law of Armed Conflict (LOAC)
Rules of Armed Engagement (ROAE)
International Military Code (IMC)

questions.question: What is the purpose of the law of war?
options: To ensure total defeat of the enemy regardless of cost
To lessen the effects of conflicts and protect combatants and noncombatants from unnecessary suffering
To provide commanders unlimited authority in the use of military force
To establish rules for declaring war between nations

questions.question: Law of war training is an obligation of the United States under provisions of which international agreements?
options: The 1907 Hague Conventions
The 1945 United Nations Charter
The 1949 Geneva Conventions, other law of war treaties, and customary international law
The 1948 Universal Declaration of Human Rights

questions.question: How many important law of war principles govern armed conflict?
options: Three
Four
Five
Six

questions.question: Which law of war principle justifies the use of all measures needed to defeat the enemy as quickly and efficiently as possible that are not prohibited by the law of war?
options: Proportionality
Distinction
Honor
Military Necessity

questions.question: Under the law of war principle of military necessity, which of the following is specifically prohibited?
options: Attacking military headquarters and supply lines
The murder of prisoners of war or execution of reprisal against specifically protected persons
Destroying enemy lines of communication
Targeting enemy military aircraft in hostile airspace

questions.question: Which law of war principle forbids the infliction of suffering, injury, or destruction unnecessary to accomplish a legitimate military purpose?
options: Military Necessity
Distinction
Humanity
Proportionality

questions.question: The law of war principle of distinction requires forces to discriminate between which of the following?
options: Officers and enlisted personnel
Active duty and reserve forces
Military forces and the civilian population, and between unprotected and protected objects
Allied and neutral nations

questions.question: Which of the following is a fundamental violation of the law of war principle of distinction?
options: Using camouflage on military equipment
Employing voluntary or involuntary human shields to protect military objectives
Conducting nighttime military operations
Engaging military targets in urban areas

questions.question: Under the law of war principle of proportionality, commanders must refrain from attacks where expected harm would be considered what?
options: Any level of incidental damage to civilians
Excessive in relation to the concrete and direct military advantage anticipated
Greater than the cost of military resources expended
Harmful to the environment surrounding the objective

questions.question: The law of war principle of honor prohibits which of the following during the conduct of hostilities?
options: Using ruses and lawful deceptions
Killing or wounding enemy persons by resort to perfidy (treachery)
Conducting surprise attacks on military objectives
Engaging in psychological operations

questions.question: If doubt exists as to whether a captured individual is a lawful combatant, noncombatant, or unprivileged belligerent, what protections do they receive?
options: No protections until status is confirmed
Protections of the Geneva Prisoner of War Convention until actual status is determined
Only basic medical treatment until a tribunal convenes
Civilian criminal justice protections of the detaining nation

questions.question: What happens to civilians who take an active or direct part in hostilities?
options: They receive enhanced protections under the Geneva Conventions
They are automatically classified as prisoners of war
They forfeit protection from being made the object of attack
They must be treated as noncombatants

questions.question: Under the law of war, airmen who parachute from a disabled aircraft and offer no resistance may be:
options: Attacked immediately as enemy combatants
Detained without any protections
Not attacked
Engaged only with non-lethal weapons

questions.question: For law of war violations, members may be prosecuted by which of the following?
options: Only civilian courts within the violator's home country
Courts-martial under the UCMJ or through an international military tribunal
The United Nations Security Council exclusively
Only the International Criminal Court

questions.question: The standing rules of engagement are approved by whom and issued by whom?
options: Approved by the Secretary of Defense and issued by combatant commanders
Approved by the U.S. President and Secretary of Defense and issued by the Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff
Approved by Congress and issued by the Secretary of the Air Force
Approved by the National Security Council and issued by theater commanders

questions.question: How many articles make up the Code of Conduct for members of the U.S. Armed Forces?
options: Four
Five
Six
Eight

questions.question: Who first published the Code of Conduct, and in what year?
options: President Harry S. Truman in 1950
President Dwight D. Eisenhower in 1955
President John F. Kennedy in 1962
President Lyndon B. Johnson in 1966

questions.question: Which president amended the Code of Conduct with gender-neutral language, and in what year?
options: President Jimmy Carter in 1980
President Ronald W. Reagan in 1988
President George H.W. Bush in 1991
President Bill Clinton in 1994

questions.question: According to Article V of the Code of Conduct, when questioned as a prisoner of war, a member is required to give which of the following?
options: Name, rank, unit assignment, and mission details
Name, rank, service number, and date of birth
Name, rank, service number, and home address
Name, rank, duty station, and commanding officer

questions.question: According to Article II of the Code of Conduct, what is the duty of a member who is isolated and no longer able to inflict casualties on the enemy?
options: To surrender voluntarily to preserve their life
To evade capture and rejoin the nearest friendly force
To destroy all equipment and wait for rescue
To negotiate terms of surrender with the enemy

questions.question: Code of Conduct training is conducted at how many levels?
options: Two
Three
Four
Five

questions.question: According to Article IV of the Code of Conduct, if the senior prisoner of war is incapacitated, who assumes command?
options: The prisoner of war with the most combat experience
The prisoner of war elected by other captives
The next senior prisoner of war
Command is deferred until the senior POW recovers

questions.question: During a terrorist hostage situation, Department of Defense policy recommends U.S. personnel should avoid which topics with their captors?
options: Weather, sports, and entertainment
Their cause, politics, or religion
Personal family information and hometown details
Military rank structure and chain of command

questions.question: According to Department of Defense policy, attempting to escape from an unfriendly government during operations other than war is:
options: Required under all circumstances per the Code of Conduct
Recommended as the first course of action
Not recommended except under life-threatening circumstances
Prohibited under all circumstances
